metadata.title: Identity & Access Admin SC-300 Practice Questions

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Azure AD (Microsoft Entra ID)?
options: Cloud-based identity and access management service
Virtual machine management platform
Database hosting service
Network traffic routing service
questions.explanation: Azure AD (now Microsoft Entra ID) is Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management service. It provides authentication and authorization for cloud and on-premises resources, SSO, MFA, and conditional access.

questions.question: What are the two types of groups available in Azure AD?
options: Security groups and Microsoft 365 groups
Distribution groups and security groups
Static groups and smart groups
User groups and device groups
questions.explanation: Azure AD supports Security groups (for managing access to shared resources) and Microsoft 365 groups (for collaboration, providing members access to a shared mailbox, calendar, files, and SharePoint site).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Azure AD device registration?
options: Provides a device identity that enables device-based Conditional Access policies
Installs antivirus software on the device
Encrypts all data on the device
Backs up the device to Azure
questions.explanation: Device registration in Azure AD creates a device identity that can be used in Conditional Access policies to control access based on device state, compliance, and trust level.

questions.question: Which Azure AD feature allows you to create groups whose membership is automatically managed based on user attributes?
options: Dynamic membership groups
Static membership groups
Assigned groups
Nested groups
questions.explanation: Dynamic membership groups use rules based on user or device attributes (like department, job title, or location) to automatically add or remove members. This eliminates the need for manual group membership management.

questions.question: What is Azure AD Connect used for?
options: Synchronizing on-premises Active Directory identities to Azure AD for hybrid identity
Connecting Azure virtual networks together
Connecting Azure AD to third-party cloud providers
Managing VPN connections to Azure
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect is the tool that synchronizes on-premises Active Directory objects (users, groups, contacts) to Azure AD, enabling hybrid identity scenarios where users have a single identity across on-premises and cloud resources.

questions.question: What is Azure AD B2B collaboration?
options: A feature that allows you to invite external users (guests) to access your organization's resources
A tool for business-to-business VPN connections
A backup solution for Azure AD data
A billing management feature
questions.explanation: Azure AD B2B collaboration allows you to invite external users (partners, vendors, contractors) as guest users in your Azure AD tenant. They authenticate with their own identity provider but access resources in your tenant.

questions.question: What is the difference between Azure AD B2B and Azure AD B2C?
options: B2B is for inviting partner/vendor users; B2C is for customer-facing identity management
B2B is for large enterprises; B2C is for small businesses
B2B requires Azure AD Premium; B2C is free
B2B supports MFA; B2C does not
questions.explanation: Azure AD B2B enables collaboration with business partners who use their own identities. Azure AD B2C is a separate identity management solution designed for consumer/customer-facing applications with social identity provider support.

questions.question: What is an Administrative Unit in Azure AD?
options: A container for Azure AD resources that restricts administrative scope to a subset of users, groups, or devices
A billing unit for Azure AD licenses
A geographic region where Azure AD is deployed
A security group with special permissions
questions.explanation: Administrative Units provide a way to delegate administrative permissions to a subset of the directory. For example, a regional IT admin can manage only users in their geographic administrative unit, not the entire tenant.

questions.question: Which three authentication methods does Azure AD Connect support for hybrid identity?
options: Password Hash Synchronization, Pass-through Authentication, and Federation with AD FS
NTLM, Kerberos, and Certificate-based Authentication
SAML, OAuth, and OpenID Connect
Basic Authentication, Digest Authentication, and Windows Integrated Authentication
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect supports three sign-in methods: Password Hash Sync (PHS) syncs password hashes to Azure AD, Pass-through Authentication (PTA) validates passwords against on-premises AD, and Federation (AD FS) delegates authentication entirely to on-premises.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Azure AD registered device and an Azure AD joined device?
options: Registered devices are personal (BYOD); joined devices are organization-owned and managed by the organization
Registered devices support Windows only; joined devices support all operating systems
Registered devices require Intune; joined devices do not
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Azure AD registered devices are typically BYOD (personal) devices where users sign in with a local or Microsoft account. Azure AD joined devices are organization-owned, signed in with an Azure AD work account, and fully managed by the organization.

questions.question: What type of Azure AD license is required to use dynamic group membership?
options: Azure AD Premium P1 or P2
Azure AD Free
Microsoft 365 Basic
No license is required
questions.explanation: Dynamic group membership rules require Azure AD Premium P1 or P2 licensing. Azure AD Free and Microsoft 365 plans only support assigned (manual) group membership.

questions.question: Your organization requires that user passwords are never synchronized to the cloud, even as hashes. Which Azure AD Connect sign-in method should you choose?
options: Pass-through Authentication (PTA) or Federation with AD FS
Password Hash Synchronization (PHS)
Seamless Single Sign-On only
Certificate-based authentication
questions.explanation: Pass-through Authentication validates passwords directly against on-premises AD without storing hashes in Azure AD. Federation with AD FS also keeps authentication entirely on-premises. PHS syncs password hashes to the cloud.

questions.question: You need to create a dynamic group that automatically includes all users in the Marketing department. Which membership rule syntax should you use?
options: (user.department -eq "Marketing")
(group.name -eq "Marketing")
(user.role -contains "Marketing")
(device.department -eq "Marketing")
questions.explanation: Dynamic group membership rules use the syntax (user.property -operator "value"). For filtering by department, the correct syntax is (user.department -eq "Marketing").

questions.question: You need to allow external partners from a specific organization to collaborate in your tenant while blocking all other external invitations. What should you configure?
options: Cross-tenant access settings with an allow list for the specific organization's tenant ID
A Conditional Access policy blocking all guest users
Azure AD B2C with social identity providers
An Azure Firewall rule blocking external domains
questions.explanation: Cross-tenant access settings in Azure AD allow you to create specific policies for partner organizations by their tenant ID. You can allow B2B collaboration with specific tenants while setting the default to block all others.

questions.question: A hybrid Azure AD joined device has both an on-premises AD computer account and an Azure AD device identity. Which tool is used to configure hybrid Azure AD join?
options: Azure AD Connect with device options configured
Microsoft Intune enrollment
Azure AD Application Proxy
Group Policy alone without Azure AD Connect
questions.explanation: Hybrid Azure AD join is configured through Azure AD Connect by enabling the device options. Azure AD Connect synchronizes the computer objects from on-premises AD to Azure AD, creating the hybrid device identity.

questions.question: Azure AD Connect Health is showing sync errors for several user objects. The error type is 'InvalidSoftMatch'. What does this typically indicate?
options: A user object in Azure AD has the same proxy address or UPN as an on-premises object but they are not properly linked
The user's password does not meet Azure AD complexity requirements
The Azure AD Connect server has run out of disk space
The on-premises domain controller is unreachable
questions.explanation: InvalidSoftMatch errors occur when Azure AD Connect tries to match an on-premises object with a cloud object using soft match attributes (like ProxyAddresses or UPN) but finds a conflict. This typically means duplicate objects need to be resolved.

questions.question: You need to delegate the ability to reset passwords for a specific department's users without granting tenant-wide Password Administrator rights. What should you use?
options: Create an Administrative Unit for the department and assign the Password Administrator role scoped to that AU
Create a custom role with password reset permissions at the tenant level
Assign the Global Administrator role to the helpdesk staff
Use Azure AD Conditional Access to limit password resets
questions.explanation: Administrative Units allow you to scope directory roles to a subset of users. By creating an AU containing the department's users and assigning Password Administrator scoped to that AU, the helpdesk can only reset passwords for those users.

questions.question: External guest users in your tenant can see the full directory of internal users by default. How do you restrict this?
options: Configure guest user access restrictions in Azure AD External Identities settings to limit guests to their own profile properties
Create a Conditional Access policy blocking Microsoft Graph for guests
Remove all guest users and use B2C instead
Enable MFA for guest users to restrict directory access
questions.explanation: Azure AD External Identities settings include guest user access restrictions that control how much of the directory guests can see. The most restrictive setting limits guests to only their own profile, hiding other users and groups entirely.

questions.question: Your organization uses Password Hash Synchronization. A user changes their on-premises AD password. How long does it typically take for the new password hash to sync to Azure AD?
options: Approximately 2 minutes (password hash sync runs on a separate, more frequent cycle than the full sync)
30 minutes (next scheduled sync cycle)
24 hours (daily sync)
Immediately in real-time
questions.explanation: Password Hash Synchronization runs on a separate cycle (approximately every 2 minutes) that is more frequent than the standard object sync cycle (every 30 minutes). This ensures password changes propagate quickly.

questions.question: You need to bulk-create 500 user accounts in Azure AD. What is the most efficient method?
options: Use the Azure AD portal's bulk create feature with a CSV file or use Microsoft Graph API/PowerShell
Create each user individually through the Azure portal
Invite all 500 users as B2B guests
Use Azure AD Connect to sync from a temporary on-premises AD
questions.explanation: Azure AD supports bulk operations through CSV upload in the portal, Microsoft Graph API, or PowerShell scripts. These methods are designed for creating large numbers of users efficiently with consistent attributes.

questions.question: Your organization wants Windows 10 devices to automatically register with Azure AD when users sign in with their work accounts. Which feature enables this?
options: Azure AD Join (for cloud-managed devices) or Hybrid Azure AD Join (for domain-joined devices)
Microsoft Intune automatic enrollment only
Azure AD Application Proxy
Windows Autopilot without any Azure AD integration
questions.explanation: Azure AD Join enables cloud-managed devices to register during OOBE or Settings. Hybrid Azure AD Join enables domain-joined devices to automatically register. Both provide the device identity needed for Conditional Access.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure AD B2B direct connect with a partner organization. How does this differ from standard B2B collaboration?
options: B2B direct connect creates a mutual trust where users access shared resources (like Teams shared channels) without being added as guests in the resource tenant
B2B direct connect requires the partner to use the same identity provider
B2B direct connect is only available for on-premises applications
B2B direct connect replaces Azure AD B2B collaboration entirely
questions.explanation: B2B direct connect establishes a mutual trust relationship between two Azure AD organizations. Users can access shared resources (like Teams shared channels) directly without being created as guest user objects in the other tenant.

questions.question: You are migrating from AD FS federation to Password Hash Synchronization for Azure AD authentication. What is this migration process called?
options: Staged rollout (converting federated domains to managed authentication)
Domain consolidation
Tenant migration
Identity federation
questions.explanation: Staged rollout allows you to migrate groups of users from federated (AD FS) authentication to managed authentication (PHS or PTA) gradually, testing the migration before converting the entire domain.

questions.question: Which Azure AD feature allows you to prevent users from being able to create new Azure AD tenants or register applications by default?
options: User settings in Azure AD that control 'Users can register applications' and tenant creation restrictions
Conditional Access policies targeting application registration
Azure Policy applied to the Azure AD resource
Network Security Group rules
questions.explanation: Azure AD User settings include toggles for 'Users can register applications' and 'Restrict non-admin users from creating tenants.' These settings control default user capabilities in the tenant.

questions.question: Your Azure AD Connect server fails permanently. You have a second server in staging mode. What is the correct recovery procedure?
options: Promote the staging server to active mode by disabling staging mode, then verify sync is working
Install Azure AD Connect on a new server from scratch; staging mode servers cannot be promoted
Restore the failed server from a backup
Contact Microsoft support to recover the sync configuration
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect supports a staging mode server that imports and synchronizes data but does not export changes. When the active server fails, you simply disable staging mode on the staging server to promote it to active, providing quick disaster recovery.

questions.question: You have 10,000 users across 5 departments. Each department has its own IT admin who should manage users only in their department. The IT admins should not be able to see or manage users in other departments. What is the most scalable solution?
options: Create 5 Administrative Units (one per department), add department users via dynamic membership rules, and assign scoped directory roles to each IT admin
Create 5 separate Azure AD tenants and use B2B collaboration between them
Assign each IT admin the User Administrator role at the tenant level with written instructions to only manage their department
Use Conditional Access to restrict admin portal access by department
questions.explanation: Administrative Units with dynamic membership rules automatically group users by department. Scoping directory roles (like User Administrator) to specific AUs ensures each IT admin can only manage their own department's users — enforced by the platform, not by policy.

questions.question: Your organization uses Azure AD B2B to collaborate with 50 partner organizations. You need to ensure that guest users from partners who leave their home organization lose access to your tenant automatically. What should you configure?
options: Cross-tenant access settings with automatic redemption and configure access reviews for guest accounts with auto-removal on denial
Manually check each partner's employee status monthly
Set all guest accounts to expire after 30 days
Use Azure AD Identity Protection to detect inactive guest accounts
questions.explanation: Access reviews can periodically verify whether guest users still need access. When configured with auto-removal, denied users are automatically removed. Cross-tenant access settings with automatic redemption ensure seamless but controlled access.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure AD Connect with a custom sync rule to filter objects so that only users in the 'CloudSync' OU are synchronized to Azure AD. What is the correct approach?
options: Configure OU-based filtering in the Azure AD Connect wizard to select only the 'CloudSync' OU
Create a Group Policy to tag users in the CloudSync OU
Set a custom attribute on each user and use attribute-based filtering in Azure AD Connect
Use Azure AD dynamic groups to filter synchronized users
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect's installation wizard includes OU-based filtering that lets you select which OUs to synchronize. This is the simplest and most supported method for controlling the sync scope by organizational unit.

questions.question: A user's Windows device is Azure AD joined. The user reports they cannot access an on-premises file share using SSO. In a hybrid environment with Azure AD Connect, what is the most likely cause?
options: The Azure AD joined device does not have line of sight to an on-premises domain controller for Kerberos authentication
Azure AD joined devices cannot access on-premises resources under any circumstances
The file share requires NTLM authentication which Azure AD does not support
The user needs to use Azure AD Application Proxy for file share access
questions.explanation: Azure AD joined devices can access on-premises resources using Kerberos SSO, but they need line of sight to a domain controller. If the device is remote without VPN access to the DC, Kerberos tickets cannot be obtained for SSO to on-premises resources.

questions.question: Your organization is creating custom security attributes in Azure AD for compliance classification (e.g., clearanceLevel, projectCode). What is unique about custom security attributes compared to extension attributes?
options: Custom security attributes can be used in Conditional Access and access control decisions; they support fine-grained RBAC for attribute access
Extension attributes are more secure because they are encrypted
Custom security attributes can only store string values
Extension attributes support Conditional Access but custom security attributes do not
questions.explanation: Custom security attributes are designed for access control scenarios — they support attribute-based access control (ABAC), can be used in Conditional Access, and have their own RBAC permissions for reading/writing. Extension attributes are general-purpose and lack these security features.

questions.question: You need to configure Azure AD B2C for a consumer-facing web application. The app should support sign-up/sign-in with Google, Facebook, and local email accounts. Which component defines the authentication flow?
options: User flows or custom policies in the B2C tenant
Conditional Access policies in the B2C tenant
Azure AD Application Proxy configuration
OAuth consent framework in the main Azure AD tenant
questions.explanation: Azure AD B2C uses user flows (for common scenarios) or custom policies (for complex scenarios) to define the identity experience — sign-up/sign-in pages, identity providers, claims collection, and token issuance behavior.

questions.question: You are planning to migrate from Azure AD Connect to Azure AD Connect Cloud Sync. What is a key architectural difference?
options: Cloud Sync uses lightweight provisioning agents instead of a full sync engine, with the sync logic running in Azure AD
Cloud Sync requires a dedicated server with SQL Server
Cloud Sync does not support multi-forest environments
Cloud Sync is an older version of Azure AD Connect being deprecated
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect Cloud Sync uses lightweight provisioning agents that are simpler to deploy (no full sync engine or SQL Server). The sync logic runs in Azure AD, making it easier to manage and more resilient to on-premises failures.

questions.question: Your security team wants to ensure that only devices with a valid TPM 2.0 chip can join Azure AD. How can you enforce this?
options: Configure a Windows Autopilot deployment profile that requires TPM 2.0 and use Conditional Access to require compliant devices
Create an Azure AD device registration policy that checks for TPM
Use Azure AD Connect to filter device objects without TPM
Manually inspect each device before Azure AD join
questions.explanation: Windows Autopilot profiles can require TPM 2.0 for device provisioning. Combined with an Intune compliance policy that checks for TPM and a Conditional Access policy requiring device compliance, you ensure only TPM-enabled devices are allowed.

questions.question: An audit reveals that multiple Azure AD user accounts have the same UPN suffix but different display names, causing confusion. The organization uses a vanity domain (contoso.com) as the primary UPN suffix. What should you implement to prevent this?
options: A naming policy for Azure AD groups and enforce UPN naming conventions through Azure AD Connect attribute transformations or provisioning logic
Delete all duplicate accounts and start over
Change all UPN suffixes to onmicrosoft.com
Use Azure AD B2B so each user has a unique external identity
questions.explanation: Azure AD group naming policies enforce naming conventions. For UPNs, you should implement attribute transformation rules in Azure AD Connect or use provisioning logic (like Power Automate or Logic Apps) to enforce consistent UPN naming standards.

questions.question: You want to allow B2B guest users to self-service sign up for your application without requiring an admin to send an invitation. What should you configure?
options: Self-service sign-up user flows in External Identities with the application registered as an API connector
Azure AD B2C user flows
Conditional Access with guest user exceptions
Azure AD Application Proxy with pre-authentication disabled
questions.explanation: Self-service sign-up user flows in External Identities allow external users to sign up for your applications without an admin invitation. You can configure identity providers, collect user attributes, and connect to custom APIs during the sign-up process.

questions.question: What is Seamless SSO in Azure AD Connect, and when is it used?
options: A feature that automatically signs in users on corporate domain-joined devices without requiring them to type their password, used with PHS or PTA
A replacement for AD FS federation
A feature that provides SSO to on-premises applications from Azure AD
A feature exclusive to Azure AD Connect Cloud Sync
questions.explanation: Seamless SSO works with PHS or PTA sign-in methods. It uses Kerberos tickets from the on-premises domain to silently authenticate domain-joined users to Azure AD cloud resources, eliminating password prompts on corporate networks.

questions.question: Your organization has a multi-forest Active Directory environment with 3 forests that do not trust each other. You need to synchronize all 3 forests to a single Azure AD tenant. What deployment architecture is required for Azure AD Connect?
options: Multiple Azure AD Connect instances (one per forest) or a single instance with multiple forest connectors
A single Azure AD Connect instance can only sync one forest; you need 3 separate Azure AD tenants
Azure AD Connect does not support multi-forest scenarios
Azure AD Connect Cloud Sync is the only option for multi-forest
questions.explanation: Azure AD Connect supports multi-forest topologies. You can use a single Azure AD Connect server with multiple Active Directory forest connectors, or deploy multiple servers. All forests sync to one Azure AD tenant with a single identity per user.

questions.question: What is the Windows Hello for Business key trust model versus the certificate trust model?
options: Key trust uses asymmetric key pairs stored in TPM; certificate trust issues PKI certificates to the device for authentication
Key trust requires a smartcard; certificate trust uses biometrics
Key trust works only in the cloud; certificate trust works only on-premises
There is no difference between the models
questions.explanation: In the key trust model, Windows Hello for Business uses asymmetric key pairs (stored in the device's TPM) that are registered with Azure AD. In the certificate trust model, a PKI certificate is issued to the device, which is used for on-premises Kerberos authentication.

questions.question: What is multi-factor authentication (MFA)?
options: A security method requiring two or more verification methods from different categories: something you know, have, or are
A method requiring users to enter their password twice
A system that uses two different passwords for authentication
A feature that requires signing in from two different devices
questions.explanation: MFA requires users to provide at least two forms of verification from different categories: something you know (password), something you have (phone, security key), or something you are (biometric like fingerprint or face).

questions.question: What is passwordless authentication in Azure AD?
options: Authentication methods that replace passwords with more secure alternatives like FIDO2 keys, Windows Hello, or the Authenticator app
Authentication that uses an empty password
Authentication that automatically generates a password for the user
Authentication that uses only biometrics without any device
questions.explanation: Passwordless authentication eliminates the password entirely, replacing it with more secure methods: FIDO2 security keys, Windows Hello for Business, or Microsoft Authenticator phone sign-in. These methods are more resistant to phishing and credential theft.

questions.question: What is Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) in Azure AD?
options: A feature that allows users to reset their own passwords without contacting the helpdesk
A tool that automatically resets passwords every 90 days
A service that generates random passwords for users
A helpdesk portal for IT admins to reset user passwords
questions.explanation: SSPR allows users to reset or change their password without administrator or helpdesk intervention. Users verify their identity through registered authentication methods (phone, email, security questions) before resetting.

questions.question: What are the three main components of a Conditional Access policy?
options: Assignments (who, what, where), Conditions (signals), and Access Controls (grant or session)
Users, Applications, and Passwords
Networks, Devices, and Certificates
Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting
questions.explanation: Conditional Access policies consist of: Assignments (which users/groups, which cloud apps, which conditions like locations/devices), and Access Controls split into Grant controls (allow/block, require MFA) and Session controls (sign-in frequency, app restrictions).

questions.question: Which Azure AD feature detects that a user's credentials may have been compromised and automatically calculates a risk level?
options: Azure AD Identity Protection
Azure AD Privileged Identity Management
Azure AD Application Proxy
Azure AD Domain Services
questions.explanation: Azure AD Identity Protection uses machine learning to detect identity-based risks like leaked credentials, anonymous IP sign-ins, atypical travel, and suspicious activities. It assigns a risk level (low, medium, high) to users and sign-ins.

questions.question: Which authentication method in Azure AD is considered the least secure for MFA?
options: SMS text message verification
FIDO2 security key
Microsoft Authenticator push notification
Windows Hello for Business
questions.explanation: SMS-based verification is considered the least secure MFA method because SMS can be intercepted via SIM swapping, SS7 attacks, or social engineering. Microsoft recommends using the Authenticator app, FIDO2, or Windows Hello instead.

questions.question: What is a Named Location in Conditional Access?
options: A defined set of IP address ranges or countries/regions used as a condition in Conditional Access policies
The physical location of the Azure AD data center
A geographical restriction on Azure resource deployment
A VPN endpoint configured in Azure
questions.explanation: Named Locations define trusted or known IP ranges and countries/regions. They are used in Conditional Access policies as conditions — for example, requiring MFA only when signing in from outside trusted locations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the combined security information registration experience in Azure AD?
options: Allows users to register for both MFA and SSPR authentication methods in a single, unified experience
Combines multiple Azure AD tenants into a single registration
Merges on-premises and cloud user accounts automatically
Registers devices and users simultaneously
questions.explanation: The combined registration experience provides a single page where users register their authentication methods for both MFA and SSPR, reducing confusion and ensuring methods are available for both scenarios.

questions.question: Azure AD Security Defaults provide a baseline level of security. Which of the following does Security Defaults enforce?
options: Requiring all users to register for MFA and blocking legacy authentication protocols
Enabling Azure AD PIM for all admin roles
Configuring Conditional Access policies automatically
Encrypting all Azure AD data at rest
questions.explanation: Security Defaults enforce basic security measures: requiring all users to register for MFA, requiring MFA for admins, blocking legacy authentication, and requiring MFA for all users when necessary. They are designed for organizations that have not configured Conditional Access.

questions.question: You need to create a Conditional Access policy that blocks access to Azure management portals from all users except Global Administrators. How should you configure the cloud app assignment?
options: Target the 'Microsoft Azure Management' cloud app and exclude the Global Administrator group from the policy assignment
Target 'All cloud apps' and create an exception for Azure management
Target the Azure portal URL in the conditions
Target the Microsoft Graph API application
questions.explanation: The 'Microsoft Azure Management' cloud app in Conditional Access covers the Azure portal, Azure PowerShell, Azure CLI, and Azure mobile app. Excluding Global Administrators from the policy assignment lets them through while blocking everyone else.

questions.question: Your organization wants to implement FIDO2 security keys for passwordless authentication. Which Azure AD license and configuration is required?
options: Enable FIDO2 security key method in the Authentication Methods policy; works with Azure AD Free for basic, P1/P2 for Conditional Access integration
Azure AD Premium P2 and a Conditional Access policy requiring FIDO2
Azure AD B2C tenant with custom policies
On-premises AD FS federation with FIDO2 plugin
questions.explanation: FIDO2 security keys are enabled in the Authentication Methods policy in Azure AD. The basic passwordless sign-in works with any license, but integrating with Conditional Access authentication strength requires Azure AD Premium P1 or P2.

questions.question: You configure SSPR for all users. A user complains they cannot reset their password. The user registered only their mobile phone number. SSPR requires two authentication methods. What is the issue?
options: The user only registered one authentication method but the policy requires two; they need to register a second method
SSPR is not enabled for the user's group
The user's account is locked out
Mobile phone is not a supported SSPR method
questions.explanation: When SSPR is configured to require two methods, users must register at least two distinct authentication methods. If the user only registered their mobile phone, they cannot satisfy the two-method requirement and will be unable to reset.

questions.question: You need to ensure that Conditional Access policies are tested before being enforced. Which policy state should you use?
options: Report-only mode
Disabled mode
Audit mode
Simulation mode
questions.explanation: Report-only mode evaluates the Conditional Access policy against sign-ins and logs the results (would have been applied/not applied) without actually enforcing the controls. This allows you to assess impact before enabling the policy.

questions.question: Your organization has both cloud-only and hybrid users. You want to enable password writeback so that cloud password changes sync back to on-premises AD. What is required?
options: Azure AD Connect with password writeback enabled and Azure AD Premium P1 or P2 license
Azure AD Free license and Azure AD Connect only
AD FS federation with password writeback plugin
Azure AD B2C with custom policies
questions.explanation: Password writeback is a feature of Azure AD Connect that writes cloud password changes (from SSPR or admin reset) back to on-premises AD. It requires Azure AD Premium P1 or P2 and must be enabled in Azure AD Connect configuration.

questions.question: Azure AD Identity Protection detects a sign-in risk of 'medium' for a user signing in from an anonymous IP address. You have a sign-in risk policy set to trigger at medium risk. What action is enforced?
options: The user is required to complete MFA before gaining access
The user's account is permanently blocked
The user is required to change their password
The user receives an email notification but is not blocked
questions.explanation: Sign-in risk policies at medium risk typically require MFA as the remediation action. This is different from user risk policies, which can require a password change. The sign-in risk triggers MFA for the suspicious sign-in attempt.

questions.question: You are configuring Conditional Access authentication strength. What does authentication strength allow you to do that standard MFA requirements cannot?
options: Specify which specific MFA methods are acceptable (e.g., only FIDO2 and WHfB, not SMS)
Require three-factor authentication
Bypass MFA for specific users
Enable passwordless for all users simultaneously
questions.explanation: Authentication strength lets you define which specific authentication method combinations satisfy the MFA requirement. For example, you can require phishing-resistant methods only (FIDO2, WHfB, certificate) rather than accepting any MFA method including SMS.

questions.question: You enable Azure AD password protection. What does this feature do?
options: Blocks users from choosing commonly used or compromised passwords, using both a global banned password list and an optional custom banned password list
Encrypts passwords stored in Azure AD with customer-managed keys
Enforces password rotation every 90 days
Blocks brute-force attacks by locking accounts after failed attempts
questions.explanation: Azure AD password protection evaluates passwords against a global banned password list (compiled from real breach data) and an optional custom list of organization-specific terms. It blocks weak and commonly compromised passwords.

questions.question: Which Identity Protection policy should you use to automatically block high-risk sign-in attempts in real-time?
options: Sign-in risk policy configured to block access at high risk level
User risk policy configured to require password change
Conditional Access policy without Identity Protection integration
Azure AD smart lockout policy
questions.explanation: The sign-in risk policy evaluates each sign-in in real-time. Configuring it to block access at high risk immediately prevents risky sign-in attempts (like impossible travel or anonymous IP usage) from succeeding.

questions.question: A user's Conditional Access policy requires a compliant device. The user has an Intune-enrolled device that was compliant yesterday but shows non-compliant today. The user is now blocked from accessing corporate resources. What should you check first?
options: The Intune compliance policy to see which specific compliance check failed on the device
Whether the user's Azure AD account has been disabled
Whether the Conditional Access policy has been modified
Whether the user's MFA registration has expired
questions.explanation: When a device changes from compliant to non-compliant, the first step is checking which Intune compliance check failed — it could be an OS update requirement, encryption check, or antivirus status. This determines the remediation path.

questions.question: Your organization wants to use certificate-based authentication (CBA) in Azure AD as the primary authentication method. What is required?
options: Upload the trusted CA certificates to Azure AD and configure the authentication methods policy to enable CBA
Deploy AD FS on-premises with certificate authentication
Install certificates on the Azure AD Connect server
Configure Azure Key Vault to store user certificates
questions.explanation: Azure AD natively supports certificate-based authentication. You upload your organization's trusted CA certificates to Azure AD, configure the CBA authentication method policy (including certificate-to-user mappings), and users authenticate with their X.509 certificates.

questions.question: Identity Protection shows multiple users with high user risk due to leaked credentials. The risk remediation policy requires a password change. However, several users are federated (using AD FS) and cannot perform SSPR through Azure AD. How do you remediate?
options: Enable password writeback in Azure AD Connect, or have the federated users change their passwords on-premises, then manually dismiss the user risk in Identity Protection
Disable the user risk policy for federated users
Delete the federated user accounts and recreate them as cloud-only
Wait for the risk to expire automatically
questions.explanation: Federated users authenticate against AD FS, so Azure AD SSPR does not directly change their password unless password writeback is configured. They must change passwords on-premises, and then an admin must dismiss the risk if it does not auto-remediate.

questions.question: You have 50 Conditional Access policies. A user reports they cannot access an application and receives a generic 'blocked by Conditional Access' error. How do you efficiently troubleshoot which policy is blocking them?
options: Use the Azure AD Sign-in logs and check the Conditional Access tab, which shows every policy evaluated and its result (success, failure, not applied) for that specific sign-in
Disable all 50 policies and re-enable them one by one
Ask the user to try different browsers and devices
Check the Azure Activity Log for Conditional Access events
questions.explanation: Azure AD Sign-in logs include a Conditional Access tab for each sign-in event that shows every policy evaluated, whether it was applied, and the specific controls that were required or blocked. This is the fastest way to identify which policy caused the block.

questions.question: Your organization is experiencing MFA fatigue attacks where attackers are repeatedly sending push notifications to users after obtaining their passwords. What should you implement?
options: Number matching in Microsoft Authenticator push notifications, requiring users to enter the number shown on the sign-in screen
Switch from push notifications to SMS verification
Disable MFA for affected users temporarily
Increase the session lifetime to reduce MFA prompts
questions.explanation: Number matching requires the user to enter a number displayed on the sign-in screen into the Authenticator app, preventing MFA fatigue attacks. Attackers cannot provide the correct number because they do not see the sign-in screen. Microsoft has made this the default for Authenticator.

questions.question: You want to implement token protection (token binding) in Conditional Access to prevent token theft and replay attacks. What does token protection achieve?
options: Binds tokens to the specific device they were issued to, making stolen tokens unusable on other devices
Encrypts all tokens with a customer-managed key
Reduces the token lifetime to 5 minutes
Requires re-authentication for every single request
questions.explanation: Token protection creates a cryptographic binding between the token and the device. If a token is stolen and replayed from a different device, the binding check fails and access is denied, mitigating token theft attacks.

questions.question: You need to implement Conditional Access policies that integrate with Microsoft Defender for Endpoint's device risk score. A device with high risk should have its access blocked to all cloud apps. How do you configure this?
options: Create a Conditional Access policy using the device filter condition to check Defender for Endpoint risk level, and set grant control to block for high-risk devices
Configure Defender for Endpoint to directly block cloud app access
Create an Intune compliance policy only without Conditional Access
Use Azure Firewall rules to block traffic from high-risk devices
questions.explanation: When Defender for Endpoint is integrated with Intune, device risk levels flow into Intune compliance. Conditional Access can then use the compliance state (which includes Defender risk level) to block access from devices assessed as high risk.

questions.question: Your organization has 15,000 users in 30 countries. You need to deploy MFA with the following requirements: phishing-resistant for executives, Authenticator app for office workers, and SMS as a fallback for field workers without smartphones. How do you implement this?
options: Create three Conditional Access policies with authentication strength: phishing-resistant for executives group, Microsoft Authenticator for office workers group, and multifactor for field workers group
Deploy a single Conditional Access policy with MFA for all users and let them choose any method
Use Security Defaults for all users
Create one authentication methods policy that enables only FIDO2 for everyone
questions.explanation: Authentication strength in Conditional Access allows targeting different groups with different method requirements. Phishing-resistant strength (FIDO2/WHfB) for executives, Authenticator strength for office workers, and standard MFA (including SMS) for field workers.

questions.question: You configure Identity Protection risk-based Conditional Access. A user with a high user risk signs in successfully and changes their password. However, the user risk remains high for several hours. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Identity Protection risk remediation can take time to process; offline risk detections may require admin review before the risk score updates
The password change failed silently
The user risk policy is misconfigured
Azure AD is experiencing a global outage
questions.explanation: Identity Protection uses both real-time and offline detections. Offline detections (like intelligence from Microsoft threat research) may not auto-remediate after password change and can require admin confirmation or take time to recalculate the risk score.

questions.question: You discover that a Conditional Access policy configured to require MFA for 'All cloud apps' is also prompting MFA for users accessing the Microsoft Authenticator app registration portal, creating a chicken-and-egg problem. How should you fix this?
options: Exclude the 'Microsoft Azure Information Protection' registration portal from the policy or use a Temporary Access Pass for initial MFA registration
Disable the Conditional Access policy entirely
Use Security Defaults instead of Conditional Access
Create a second tenant for MFA registration
questions.explanation: Temporary Access Pass (TAP) provides a time-limited passcode that satisfies MFA requirements, allowing users to complete initial Authenticator registration. Alternatively, excluding the registration portal from the MFA policy breaks the circular dependency.

questions.question: What is a Temporary Access Pass (TAP) in Azure AD?
options: A time-limited passcode issued by an admin that satisfies strong authentication requirements for onboarding passwordless credentials
A permanent password assigned to temporary workers
A VPN access token for temporary contractors
A one-time backup code generated by the user
questions.explanation: TAP is a time-limited passcode issued by an administrator that satisfies MFA requirements. It is primarily used for onboarding users to passwordless methods like FIDO2 or Windows Hello, solving the bootstrapping problem.

questions.question: You want to prevent users from accessing corporate data from unmanaged web browsers while still allowing access from managed devices. Which Conditional Access session control should you use?
options: Use app-enforced restrictions (for Exchange Online and SharePoint) or Conditional Access App Control with Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps
Block all browser access entirely
Require device compliance only without session controls
Use an Azure Firewall rule to block browser traffic
questions.explanation: App-enforced restrictions work with Exchange Online and SharePoint to limit functionality in unmanaged browsers (e.g., block downloads). Conditional Access App Control with Defender for Cloud Apps provides similar controls for a broader set of apps.

questions.question: How does Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) improve security over standard token-based access?
options: CAE allows resource providers to evaluate critical events in near real-time and revoke access without waiting for the token to expire
CAE encrypts all access tokens with customer-managed keys
CAE eliminates the need for refresh tokens entirely
CAE extends token lifetimes to reduce authentication frequency
questions.explanation: With standard tokens, a disabled user retains access until the token expires (up to 1 hour). CAE enables resource providers to subscribe to critical Azure AD events (account disabled, password changed, location changed) and evaluate tokens in near real-time.

questions.question: Your organization has smart lockout configured in Azure AD. Users in a specific office are being locked out frequently due to a legacy application that uses cached credentials. How should you resolve this?
options: Configure a custom smart lockout threshold for the affected location using Named Locations, and migrate the legacy application to modern authentication
Disable smart lockout entirely for all users
Increase the global lockout threshold to 999
Create separate Azure AD accounts for the legacy application
questions.explanation: Smart lockout can be configured with custom thresholds. However, the root cause fix is migrating the legacy application to modern authentication (OAuth/OIDC) so it does not repeatedly fail with cached credentials. The threshold adjustment is a short-term mitigation.

questions.question: You need to ensure that SSPR password changes also update the on-premises Active Directory password for hybrid users. What two things must be configured?
options: Enable password writeback in Azure AD Connect and enable SSPR in Azure AD with the appropriate user group
Enable password hash synchronization and disable SSPR
Deploy AD FS and configure SSPR through AD FS
Install the Azure AD Password Protection proxy on-premises
questions.explanation: Password writeback in Azure AD Connect enables cloud password changes to flow back to on-premises AD. SSPR must also be enabled for the target users. Both components are required for hybrid SSPR to work end-to-end.

questions.question: What is the 'What If' tool in Conditional Access used for?
options: Simulating which Conditional Access policies would apply to a specific user under specified conditions without actually signing in
Creating new Conditional Access policies from templates
Rolling back Conditional Access policy changes
Generating compliance reports for Conditional Access
questions.explanation: The What If tool lets you simulate a sign-in scenario by specifying a user, application, location, device platform, and other conditions. It shows which policies would be applied, helping you validate policy behavior before deployment.

questions.question: A compliance requirement mandates that MFA verification must be performed by a hardware token (not phone-based). Which Azure AD authentication methods satisfy this requirement?
options: FIDO2 security keys and hardware OATH tokens
Microsoft Authenticator push notification and TOTP
SMS and voice call
Windows Hello for Business (with software TPM)
questions.explanation: FIDO2 security keys are physical hardware tokens that provide phishing-resistant authentication. Hardware OATH tokens generate time-based one-time passwords from a physical device. Both satisfy hardware-only MFA requirements.

questions.question: Your security team wants to detect and respond when a user's registered authentication methods are changed by an attacker (e.g., adding a new phone number). How can you detect this?
options: Monitor the AuditLogs in Azure AD for authentication method registration changes and create alerts for changes from suspicious IPs or from risky users
Check the user's profile page weekly for changes
Require admin approval for all authentication method changes
Disable self-service authentication method registration
questions.explanation: Azure AD Audit Logs record authentication method registrations and changes. By monitoring these logs (in Sentinel or Azure Monitor) and alerting on changes from unusual IPs or for users with elevated risk, you can detect MFA method manipulation by attackers.

questions.question: You are implementing a Global Secure Access (Microsoft Entra Internet Access) solution. How does this relate to Conditional Access?
options: Global Secure Access integrates with Conditional Access to apply identity-aware network security controls, enabling policies based on both identity signals and network context
Global Secure Access replaces Conditional Access entirely
They are unrelated features that cannot be used together
Global Secure Access only works with on-premises applications
questions.explanation: Global Secure Access (Microsoft Entra Internet Access and Private Access) extends Conditional Access to the network layer. It enables compliant network conditions in Conditional Access policies, combining identity-aware and network-aware security controls.

questions.question: What is an enterprise application in Azure AD?
options: A representation of an application that users in your organization can sign in to using Azure AD credentials
A type of Azure virtual machine
A billing entity for Azure subscriptions
A network security group for application traffic
questions.explanation: Enterprise applications in Azure AD are service principals that represent applications users can access. They include SaaS apps from the gallery, custom-developed apps, and on-premises apps published through Application Proxy.

questions.question: What is the Azure AD Application Gallery?
options: A catalog of pre-integrated SaaS applications with built-in SSO and provisioning configurations
A marketplace for purchasing Azure services
A collection of Azure VM images
A repository of PowerShell scripts
questions.explanation: The Azure AD Application Gallery contains thousands of pre-integrated SaaS applications with ready-made SSO configurations (SAML, OpenID Connect, or password-based). Many also include automatic user provisioning (SCIM) support.

questions.question: What is the difference between delegated permissions and application permissions in Azure AD?
options: Delegated permissions act on behalf of a signed-in user; application permissions act as the app itself without a signed-in user
Delegated permissions require admin consent; application permissions require user consent
Delegated permissions are more powerful than application permissions
There is no difference; they are interchangeable terms
questions.explanation: Delegated permissions are used when a signed-in user is present — the app acts on behalf of the user with their permissions. Application permissions are used in daemon/background services where no user is signed in — the app acts with its own identity.

questions.question: What is Azure AD Application Proxy used for?
options: Providing secure remote access to on-premises web applications without a VPN
Proxying traffic between Azure virtual networks
Caching application content at edge locations
Load balancing traffic between Azure App Services
questions.explanation: Azure AD Application Proxy enables secure remote access to on-premises web applications through Azure AD. Users authenticate to Azure AD and traffic flows through the Application Proxy connector without requiring a VPN or exposing the application directly to the internet.

questions.question: What does SSO (Single Sign-On) in Azure AD provide?
options: Users sign in once to Azure AD and access multiple applications without re-entering credentials
Users use a single password across all personal and work accounts
Only one user can sign in to an application at a time
Applications share a single service account for authentication
questions.explanation: SSO allows users to authenticate once to Azure AD and then access all configured applications without additional sign-in prompts. Azure AD supports SSO through SAML, OpenID Connect, password-based, and linked SSO methods.

questions.question: What is the consent framework in Azure AD?
options: A mechanism that allows users or admins to grant applications permission to access resources on their behalf
A legal agreement between Azure AD and the application developer
A tool for managing software licenses
A compliance framework for data privacy
questions.explanation: The Azure AD consent framework presents a permission request to users or admins when an application needs access to resources (like reading user profiles or sending email). User consent grants permissions for that user; admin consent grants permissions for all users.

questions.question: Which SSO protocol is most commonly used with modern cloud applications integrated with Azure AD?
options: OpenID Connect (OIDC)
NTLM
Kerberos
Basic Authentication
questions.explanation: OpenID Connect (OIDC) is the modern authentication protocol built on OAuth 2.0 that is most commonly used for SSO with cloud applications. It provides both authentication and authorization with ID tokens and access tokens.

questions.question: What is the difference between an app registration and an enterprise application in Azure AD?
options: An app registration defines the application's identity configuration; an enterprise application is the service principal instance in your tenant
App registrations are for Microsoft apps; enterprise applications are for custom apps
They are the same thing with different names
App registrations require a paid license; enterprise applications are free
questions.explanation: An app registration is the application's identity definition (client ID, redirect URIs, permissions). An enterprise application (service principal) is the local instance of that application in your tenant, controlling who can access it and what permissions it has.

questions.question: What happens when an admin grants 'admin consent' for an application's permissions?
options: The permissions are granted for all users in the organization; individual users will not see consent prompts
Only the admin who consented can use the application
The application gets global administrator privileges
The application's permissions are valid for 24 hours only
questions.explanation: Admin consent grants the requested permissions on behalf of the entire organization. All users can access the application without individual consent prompts. This is especially important for application permissions that always require admin consent.

questions.question: In a SAML-based SSO configuration, what is the role of Azure AD?
options: Identity Provider (IdP) that authenticates the user and issues SAML assertions to the application
Service Provider (SP) that hosts the application
Certificate Authority that issues SSL certificates
DNS provider for the application's domain
questions.explanation: In SAML-based SSO, Azure AD acts as the Identity Provider (IdP). When a user accesses an application (Service Provider/SP), Azure AD authenticates the user and sends a SAML assertion containing the user's identity and attributes to the application.

questions.question: What is SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) used for in Azure AD?
options: Automatic user provisioning and deprovisioning between Azure AD and SaaS applications
Single sign-on protocol between Azure AD and applications
Encrypting data between Azure AD and applications
Managing application licenses in Azure AD
questions.explanation: SCIM is an open standard protocol that Azure AD uses to automatically provision (create), update, and deprovision (disable/delete) user accounts in SaaS applications, keeping identities synchronized between Azure AD and the target app.

questions.question: Your security team wants to prevent users from granting consent to applications that request high-risk permissions like reading all users' mailboxes. What should you configure?
options: Configure the user consent settings to allow consent only for apps from verified publishers and for low-impact permissions; require admin consent for everything else
Disable all application consent for users and admins
Block all third-party applications from the tenant
Enable MFA for all application consent requests
questions.explanation: Azure AD user consent settings can be configured to allow users to consent only to apps from verified publishers requesting specific low-risk permission sets. High-risk permissions automatically require admin consent, preventing consent phishing attacks.

questions.question: You are configuring SAML-based SSO for a third-party application. The application requires a specific claim format for the NameID. Where do you configure this?
options: In the enterprise application's SSO settings under the 'Attributes & Claims' section
In the application registration's manifest file
In the Conditional Access policy for the application
In the Azure AD tenant's global settings
questions.explanation: The Attributes & Claims section of an enterprise application's SAML SSO configuration allows you to customize the NameID format (email, UPN, persistent, etc.) and add additional claims that the application requires.

questions.question: You need to ensure that only users in specific groups can access a SaaS application configured in Azure AD. How do you restrict access?
options: Enable the 'Assignment required' property on the enterprise application and assign only the allowed groups
Create a Conditional Access policy to block non-members
Remove the application from the Azure AD gallery
Configure the application's own internal access controls only
questions.explanation: Setting 'Assignment required' to Yes on an enterprise application means only users and groups explicitly assigned to the application can access it. Without assignment, the user receives an error when attempting to sign in.

questions.question: An application registered in Azure AD uses a client secret for authentication. The secret is expiring in 7 days. What should you do?
options: Generate a new client secret, update the application configuration, and plan to migrate to certificates or managed identities for better security
Extend the expiration date of the current secret
Delete the application and recreate it with a new secret
Ignore it; Azure AD will auto-renew the secret
questions.explanation: Client secrets cannot be extended — you must create a new one. Update the application's configuration with the new secret before the old one expires. For improved security, consider migrating to certificate credentials or managed identities, which are more secure.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure AD Application Proxy for an on-premises web application. The application uses Windows Integrated Authentication (Kerberos). What must you configure for SSO to work?
options: Kerberos Constrained Delegation (KCD) on the Application Proxy connector's computer account in Active Directory
Password-based SSO in the enterprise application settings
SAML federation between Azure AD and the on-premises application
Certificate-based authentication on the Application Proxy connector
questions.explanation: For on-premises apps using Kerberos/Windows Integrated Authentication, you configure KCD on the Application Proxy connector. The connector impersonates the user to obtain a Kerberos ticket, enabling SSO without the user re-entering credentials.

questions.question: You need to automatically provision and deprovision user accounts from Azure AD to a SaaS application. Which provisioning mode should you configure?
options: Automatic provisioning using the Azure AD provisioning service (SCIM-based)
Manual provisioning by an administrator
Just-in-time provisioning through SAML assertions
Self-service provisioning through the My Apps portal
questions.explanation: Automatic provisioning uses the Azure AD provisioning service to synchronize user accounts to the SaaS app via SCIM. It creates, updates, and disables accounts based on scope rules and attribute mappings, running on a regular cycle.

questions.question: You notice that users are consenting to suspicious third-party applications that request broad permissions. An admin consent workflow is not configured. What should you implement?
options: Enable the admin consent workflow so that users can request admin approval for apps they want to use, and admins can review and approve or deny
Block all third-party applications permanently
Deploy a web application firewall to block app consent requests
Send a company-wide email asking users to stop consenting to apps
questions.explanation: The admin consent workflow allows users to request admin approval for applications that require permissions beyond what they are allowed to consent to. Admins receive notifications and can review, approve, or deny requests, preventing shadow IT.

questions.question: You need to configure SSO for an application that only supports header-based authentication. Which Azure AD feature supports this?
options: Azure AD Application Proxy with header-based SSO (or integration with PingAccess for complex scenarios)
SAML-based SSO configuration
OpenID Connect SSO configuration
Password-based SSO with credential vaulting
questions.explanation: Some legacy applications authenticate using HTTP headers. Azure AD Application Proxy supports header-based SSO natively for simple scenarios and integrates with PingAccess for complex header-based authentication requirements.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'My Apps' portal in Azure AD?
options: A web-based portal where users can discover and launch applications they have been assigned to
An administrative portal for managing Azure AD settings
A developer portal for registering applications
A support portal for creating help desk tickets
questions.explanation: The My Apps portal (myapps.microsoft.com) provides users with a personalized launchpad showing all applications they have been assigned to. It supports SSO launch, self-service group management, and access request workflows.

questions.question: An app registration has both 'User.Read' (delegated) and 'User.Read.All' (application) permissions configured. When a user signs in to the app interactively, which permissions apply?
options: Only the delegated permission (User.Read) applies because a signed-in user is present
Both permissions apply simultaneously
Only the application permission (User.Read.All) applies
Neither permission applies until admin consent is granted
questions.explanation: When a user signs in interactively, the delegated permissions are used because a user context exists. Application permissions are used only when the app runs as a daemon/service without a signed-in user. Both types can be configured but apply in different contexts.

questions.question: You are troubleshooting a SAML-based SSO failure. The application returns an error: 'SAML Response signature validation failed.' What is the most likely cause?
options: The SAML signing certificate in Azure AD has been rotated but the application still has the old certificate configured
The user does not have MFA enabled
The application requires OpenID Connect instead of SAML
The user's Azure AD password has expired
questions.explanation: SAML responses are signed with a certificate. If Azure AD's signing certificate is rotated (auto-rotation or manual), the application must be updated with the new certificate. A mismatch causes signature validation failure.

questions.question: You configure automatic user provisioning to a SaaS application. Users are created successfully, but attribute mappings for 'department' and 'manager' are showing incorrect values. Where should you investigate?
options: Review the attribute mappings in the provisioning configuration and check if the source attributes in Azure AD are populated correctly
Reconfigure the SSO settings for the application
Delete and recreate all user accounts in the SaaS application
Reinstall the provisioning agent
questions.explanation: Provisioning attribute mappings define how Azure AD attributes map to the target application's attributes. Incorrect values typically mean either the source attribute in Azure AD is empty/wrong or the mapping expression is misconfigured.

questions.question: Your organization's security audit finds 200+ application registrations with unused or excessive API permissions. Many were created by developers who have since left. What is the best remediation approach?
options: Review each application's sign-in logs and audit logs, remove permissions that are not actively being used, and delete abandoned app registrations
Delete all 200+ application registrations immediately
Revoke admin consent for all applications globally
Block all application registrations going forward
questions.explanation: Reviewing sign-in and audit logs reveals which applications are actively used and which permissions they exercise. You can then safely remove unused permissions (principle of least privilege) and delete abandoned registrations without disrupting active applications.

questions.question: You need to publish an on-premises REST API through Azure AD Application Proxy so that a SPA (Single Page Application) in the cloud can call it. The SPA uses MSAL to acquire tokens. What challenge must you address?
options: Configure CORS on the Application Proxy and ensure the SPA acquires tokens for the Application Proxy's service principal (not the backend API directly)
Application Proxy does not support REST APIs
The SPA must use a VPN to access the API instead
Application Proxy only supports SAML-based applications
questions.explanation: Application Proxy can publish REST APIs. The SPA must acquire an access token for the Application Proxy's app registration (service principal). CORS must be configured on the backend to allow the Application Proxy origin. The connector handles forwarding the authenticated request.

questions.question: You need to implement a multi-tenant application that allows users from any Azure AD tenant to sign in. What endpoint and configuration should the application use?
options: Use the /common or /organizations endpoint in Azure AD and set 'Supported account types' to 'Accounts in any organizational directory'
Create a separate app registration in every customer tenant
Use the tenant-specific endpoint with the developer's tenant ID
Configure B2B collaboration with each customer tenant
questions.explanation: Multi-tenant applications use the /common or /organizations endpoint, which accepts tokens from any Azure AD tenant. The app registration must be configured with 'Accounts in any organizational directory' as the supported account type.

questions.question: A developer creates an app registration with the 'Mail.ReadWrite' application permission and grants admin consent. The app can now read and write all users' mailboxes. How should you limit the app's access to only specific mailboxes?
options: Configure an application access policy in Exchange Online that scopes the app's access to a specific mail-enabled security group
Revoke admin consent and use delegated permissions instead
Create a Conditional Access policy targeting the application
This cannot be limited; application permissions always apply to all users
questions.explanation: Exchange Online application access policies allow you to scope application permissions (like Mail.ReadWrite) to specific mailboxes by associating the app with a mail-enabled security group. Members of the group are the only mailboxes the app can access.

questions.question: An Application Proxy connector group has two connectors. One connector fails, and users report intermittent access failures to the published application. What is the expected behavior?
options: The remaining active connector handles all traffic; intermittent failures suggest the failed connector had not been fully removed from rotation yet
Both connectors must be active for the application to work
Application Proxy automatically deploys a replacement connector
The application becomes completely inaccessible
questions.explanation: Application Proxy performs load balancing across connectors in a group. When a connector fails, traffic shifts to the remaining connector(s). Brief intermittent failures may occur during the failover period until health checks remove the failed connector from the pool.

questions.question: You need to configure an OAuth 2.0 on-behalf-of (OBO) flow for a middle-tier API that calls a downstream API using the signed-in user's identity. What is required in the middle-tier API's app registration?
options: The middle-tier API must have a delegated permission for the downstream API and use the OBO flow to exchange the incoming token for a new token scoped to the downstream API
The middle-tier API must use application permissions for the downstream API
The middle-tier API must forward the original access token directly to the downstream API
The middle-tier API must use its own client credentials to call the downstream API
questions.explanation: The OBO flow allows a middle-tier API to exchange the access token it received from the client for a new access token that is scoped to the downstream API. The middle-tier app needs delegated permissions for the downstream API configured in its registration.

questions.question: Your organization wants to monitor for consent grant attacks where a malicious application tricks an admin into granting broad permissions. What monitoring should you implement?
options: Alert on Azure AD Audit Logs for 'Consent to application' events where high-risk permissions (like Mail.ReadWrite, Directory.ReadWrite.All) are granted
Monitor the Azure Activity Log for resource creation events
Check the application gallery weekly for new applications
Enable Defender for Cloud on the Azure AD resource
questions.explanation: Consent grant attacks (illicit consent grant) involve tricking admins into consenting to malicious apps with broad permissions. Monitoring audit logs for consent events involving high-risk permissions enables detection. Microsoft Sentinel has built-in analytics rules for this.

questions.question: You are configuring Application Proxy for an on-premises application. The application is behind a load balancer with multiple backend servers. Where should you install the Application Proxy connectors?
options: On servers in the same network as the backend application servers, with line of sight to the load balancer
On the Azure AD Connect server
On the load balancer itself
On any internet-facing server in the DMZ
questions.explanation: Application Proxy connectors should be installed close to the backend application servers with network access to the load balancer or application endpoints. They make outbound connections to Azure and require no inbound ports, so they do not need to be in the DMZ.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'verified publisher' status for an Azure AD application?
options: Indicates the app publisher's identity has been verified by Microsoft using their MPN (Microsoft Partner Network) account, helping users trust the application
Indicates the application has passed a security audit by Microsoft
Indicates the application is a first-party Microsoft application
Indicates the application is free of charge
questions.explanation: A verified publisher badge indicates the app developer has verified their identity through the Microsoft Partner Network. This helps users and admins determine whether an application requesting consent is from a trustworthy source.

questions.question: An application needs to call the Microsoft Graph API to create users in Azure AD. Which minimum permission should be granted?
options: User.ReadWrite.All (application permission) with admin consent
User.Read (delegated permission)
Directory.ReadWrite.All (application permission)
User.ReadBasic.All (delegated permission)
questions.explanation: Creating users requires write access to user objects. User.ReadWrite.All (application permission) is the minimum permission for creating users programmatically. Directory.ReadWrite.All is more than needed. User.Read is insufficient for user creation.

questions.question: You configured password-based SSO for an application in Azure AD. Users report that they need to enter their application credentials the first time. What happens on subsequent sign-ins?
options: Azure AD stores the credentials and automatically submits them on subsequent sign-ins (via the My Apps browser extension)
Users must enter their credentials every time
The application generates a unique token for each sign-in
Azure AD converts password-based SSO to SAML SSO after the first sign-in
questions.explanation: Password-based SSO (credential vaulting) stores the user's application credentials securely in Azure AD. On subsequent sign-ins via the My Apps portal or browser extension, Azure AD automatically fills in and submits the credentials.

questions.question: You need to migrate an application from ADFS to Azure AD for SSO. The application uses SAML 2.0 and relies on custom claim rules in ADFS that transform user attributes. What tool helps assess migration readiness?
options: The AD FS application migration tool in Azure AD, which analyzes ADFS relying party trusts and identifies migration compatibility
Azure AD Connect Health only
Microsoft Assessment and Planning Toolkit
Azure Migrate service
questions.explanation: The AD FS application migration tool analyzes your ADFS relying party trust configurations and identifies which applications can be migrated to Azure AD, flagging any unsupported claim rules or features that need alternatives.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'app roles' feature in Azure AD application registrations?
options: Define custom roles for the application that can be assigned to users, groups, or service principals for application-level authorization
Assign Azure RBAC roles to the application
Define API permissions the application needs
Configure SSO protocols for the application
questions.explanation: App roles are custom roles defined in the app registration manifest. They appear in the access token's 'roles' claim and allow the application to implement its own role-based authorization (e.g., Admin, Reader, Approver) using Azure AD as the source.

questions.question: Your Application Proxy connector must authenticate to an on-premises application that uses SAML for authentication (the app is a SAML SP). Azure AD is the IdP. How does this work?
options: Azure AD performs pre-authentication, issues a SAML assertion, and the connector passes it to the on-premises SAML SP application
Application Proxy does not support on-premises SAML applications
The connector acts as a secondary IdP for the on-premises application
You must convert the on-premises application to use OpenID Connect
questions.explanation: Application Proxy supports pre-authentication with Azure AD for on-premises SAML applications. Azure AD acts as the IdP, authenticates the user, creates a SAML assertion, and the Application Proxy connector delivers it to the on-premises SAML application (SP).

questions.question: You are implementing a zero-trust architecture and want to ensure that no application can access any API without explicit permission grants. Currently, some applications have overly broad permissions. What governance approach should you take?
options: Conduct an app permissions audit, implement the principle of least privilege for each app, restrict user consent to verified publishers with low-risk permissions, and establish an admin consent workflow
Block all API access for all applications
Delete all app registrations and start from scratch
Disable the Microsoft Graph API for the tenant
questions.explanation: A zero-trust approach to app permissions involves auditing existing permissions, removing excessive grants, restricting user consent scope, and implementing admin consent workflows. This ensures every app has only the minimum permissions needed.

questions.question: What is Azure AD entitlement management?
options: A feature that automates access request, assignment, review, and expiration for groups, applications, and SharePoint sites
A billing management feature for Azure AD licenses
A tool for managing Azure resource entitlements
A compliance reporting tool
questions.explanation: Entitlement management provides access packages that bundle groups, applications, and SharePoint sites. Users can request access, managers can approve, and access automatically expires — automating the identity governance lifecycle.

questions.question: What is an Azure AD access review?
options: A periodic review process that validates whether users still need access to groups, applications, or roles
A security scan of Azure AD for vulnerabilities
A report of all sign-in activities in the last 30 days
A review of Azure AD configuration settings
questions.explanation: Access reviews enable organizations to periodically verify that users still need their current access to groups, applications, or Azure AD roles. Reviewers can approve or deny continued access, and denied access can be automatically removed.

questions.question: What is Azure AD Privileged Identity Management (PIM)?
options: A service that manages, controls, and monitors access to important resources using just-in-time and just-enough-access principles
A password management tool for privileged accounts
A network security service for protecting Azure resources
A backup service for Azure AD data
